item.title: Is the UK enjoying a productivity rebound?
item.description: One way to find out would be to test if faster growth and lower unemployment are possible without inflation

item.title: Don’t exclude Yemen from the Gulf’s new economic map
item.description: It may be unstable, but the country is central to the region’s future — and leaving it out will spark more violence

item.title: What happened to the great rebirth of American manufacturing?
item.description: Skills shortages, shifting tariffs and complex permitting processes are major barriers

item.title: How tin went from baked beans to AI gravy train
item.description: Higher prices are prompting some miners to take up their shovels

item.title: Anthropic leans into AI’s nascent slice-and-dice era
item.description: Clever financial engineering is allowing conservative, risk-averse investors to participate enthusiastically

item.title: How Northern Ireland became a breeding ground for riots
item.description: Violence erupts in a repeat of 2025 disorder, aided by tendency for many politicians to be equivocal on the crimes

item.title: Gold and bitcoin: the assets the rally forgot
item.description: Gold and ‘digital gold’ both feel like yesterday’s Fomo trades

item.title: Why the world must agree to regulate AI
item.description: A technological disarmament pact between the US and China would make everyone feel more secure

item.title: Homeowners are getting used to the UK’s perma-crisis
item.description: Households appear to have adapted to a constant state of flux

item.title: The Fed might yet again take the punch bowl away
item.description: Potential rate rises could give way to a broad range of investment opportunities

item.title: How did air conditioning become a heated debate?
item.description: It’s illogical to argue that heating buildings to a safe temperature is a basic right but cooling them is an affectation

item.title: Who owns my name after I sell my business?
item.description: I want to use my name for new products, but my former backers are threatening legal action

item.title: The pension system missed Gen X women — what if they are the future?
item.description: A sandwich generation caught between two very different savings systems

item.title: The hard truths about England’s receding coastline
item.description: The entire shoreline cannot be protected from erosion, but the state can mitigate future fallout

item.title: Do black holes do more than devour matter?
item.description: The supermassive one at the centre of our galaxy is surprisingly windy — spitting material out as well as taking it in

item.title: GSK’s $10.6bn cancer deal will lead to more
item.description: Making additional moves would be in keeping with pharma group’s strategy of building its pipeline as fast as possible

item.title: The Fed is going to have to rethink its global role
item.description: Stabilising the finances of another country is a foreign policy decision as well as an economic one

item.title: Lessons from Germany’s (entirely predictable) UN Security Council defeat
item.description: In a world of aggressive great powers, middle powers must learn to forge alliances

item.title: Trump is becoming Jimmy Carter
item.description: Like the 39th US president, the current occupant of the White House has handed control of the narrative to Iran

item.title: Real Madrid and the dangers of mixing fans and finance
item.description: Club president Florentino Pérez’s aim of bringing in outside investors is high risk

item.title: Will Starmer fight or fold?
item.description: No 10 tries to gauge support for PM as failing to secure backers would be embarrassing and suggest he can’t govern

item.title: We’ve died and gone to volatility heaven
item.description: Plus non-dollar stablecoins

item.title: Why are we still arguing about the industrial revolution?
item.description: Historical data does not offer much insight about how our predecessors navigated profound change

item.title: The film industry needs franchises like Star Wars
item.description: Blockbusters keep cinemas open and leave room for quirkier originals to thrive

item.title: There is a simpler option for making AI pay its way: tax it properly
item.description: The world of laissez-faire no longer exists given the impact of the technology
